sources.title: The Cold War: The Berlin Wall (Grade 12)
Grade 12 - The impact of the Cold War in forming the world as it was in the 1960s
Grade 12 - The end of the Cold War and a new global world order, 1989 to present
Timeline: The end of the Cold War and a new world order

slides.heading: The Cold War
slides.lead: How two superpowers, without fighting each other directly, divided the world into rival camps and shaped global politics for almost half a century.

image.alt: A globe split down the middle into two halves, one red and one blue, symbolising a world divided between two superpowers.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><clipPath id='g'><circle cx='160' cy='90' r='68'/></clipPath><g clip-path='url(#g)'><rect x='92' y='22' width='68' height='136' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='160' y='22' width='68' height='136' fill='#2f4b7a'/></g><circle cx='160' cy='90' r='68' fill='none' stroke='#3a3a3a' stroke-width='3'/><rect x='158' y='22' width='4' height='136' fill='#f4efe6'/></svg>

slides.notes: Open by clarifying the paradox in the name: a 'war' that was mostly *cold* - fought through ideology, propaganda, spying, an arms race and proxy conflicts rather than direct battle between the USA and USSR. Set the timeframe: roughly 1945 to 1991. Ask learners why two former wartime allies became enemies - the driving question of this lesson.

slides.heading: What was the Cold War?
slides.lead: A prolonged state of political and military tension between the United States and the Soviet Union and their allies.

blocks.text: The Cold War was a global rivalry between two **superpowers** - the capitalist **United States (USA)** and the communist **Soviet Union (USSR)** - from the end of the Second World War in 1945 until the collapse of the USSR in 1991. It was 'cold' because the two never fought each other directly; open war between them was deterred by nuclear weapons.

items: A **bipolar order**: most nations aligned with one of two power blocs.
The conflict played out through ideology, economics, propaganda, espionage and an arms race.
It was fought in 'proxy wars' - conflicts in other countries backed by the superpowers.

slides.notes: Define the essential vocabulary learners need for the whole unit: superpower, bipolar, capitalism, communism, proxy war, arms race, deterrence. Stress that 'bipolar' means two poles or centres of power. Explain that fear of mutual nuclear destruction is what kept the war 'cold' - a theme returned to on the arms race and Cuba slides.

slides.heading: From allies to enemies
slides.lead: The USA and USSR defeated Nazi Germany together, then fell out over how to remake the post-war world.

blocks.text: During the Second World War the USA, Britain and the USSR were allies against Hitler. As victory neared, their leaders met at **Yalta (February 1945)** and **Potsdam (July-August 1945)** to plan the peace - but deep disagreements surfaced over the future of Eastern Europe, especially Poland and defeated Germany.

items: The USA wanted free elections and open markets across Europe.
The USSR, invaded twice by Germany, wanted friendly buffer states on its borders.
Mutual suspicion deepened: the USSR distrusted the US atomic bomb; the USA distrusted Soviet expansion.

image.alt: Two hands that once shook now pulling apart, with a jagged crack opening between them.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='30' y='72' width='70' height='16' rx='8' fill='#2f4b7a'/><rect x='140' y='72' width='70' height='16' rx='8' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M118 40 L126 70 L114 82 L124 96 L116 120' fill='none' stroke='#3a3a3a' stroke-width='4' stroke-linejoin='round'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain that historians debate who was 'to blame' for the Cold War (orthodox, revisionist and post-revisionist schools). Encourage learners to see it as a clash of interests AND ideologies, not one villain. Highlight the security dilemma: each side's defensive moves looked aggressive to the other. [SME] Confirm the depth of detail expected on historiographical debate for your specific CAPS assessment.

slides.heading: Two ways of ordering society
slides.lead: At its heart the Cold War was a contest between two opposed visions of politics and economics.

blocks.heading: Capitalism (USA)
blocks.text: Private ownership, free markets, multi-party democracy and individual freedoms.

blocks.heading: Communism (USSR)
blocks.text: State ownership, a planned economy, one-party rule and collective goals over individual choice.

blocks.heading: Each claimed to be the future
blocks.text: Both blocs promoted their system as superior and tried to win over the newly independent nations of Africa and Asia.

slides.notes: Keep the comparison balanced and analytical: describe each system on its own terms rather than caricaturing either. Note that in practice both blocs fell short of their ideals - the USA supported some undemocratic regimes, and the USSR was repressive. This nuance matters for the FET-level skill of evaluating bias and reliability in sources.

slides.heading: Containment: the Truman Doctrine and Marshall Plan
slides.lead: The USA committed itself to stopping the spread of communism beyond where it already existed.

blocks.text: In March 1947 President **Harry Truman** pledged US support for 'free peoples' resisting communism - the policy of **containment**. In 1948 the **Marshall Plan** followed, offering billions of dollars to rebuild war-torn Western Europe, both to relieve suffering and to make communism less attractive.

items: The USSR saw the Marshall Plan as US 'dollar imperialism' and forbade Eastern bloc states to join.
Military alliances hardened the divide: **NATO** (Western, 1949) and the **Warsaw Pact** (Eastern, 1955).
Europe was now split into two rival, armed camps.

image.alt: A shield labelled with a dollar sign standing between arrows pushing in from the right.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M70 30 L120 30 L120 90 Q120 120 95 132 Q70 120 70 90 Z' fill='#2f4b7a'/><text x='95' y='90' font-size='42' fill='#f4efe6' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif'>$</text><g fill='#8a1f1f'><path d='M210 60 L170 60 L170 50 L150 70 L170 90 L170 80 L210 80 Z'/><path d='M210 100 L170 100 L170 90 L150 110 L170 130 L170 120 L210 120 Z'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Draw out cause and effect: containment and the Marshall Plan were US responses to perceived Soviet expansion, and they in turn provoked Soviet counter-moves (Cominform, later the Warsaw Pact). This action-reaction dynamic is the engine of the early Cold War. Note the Marshall Plan's mixed motives - humanitarian relief and strategic self-interest.

slides.heading: The Iron Curtain
slides.lead: Europe was split between a Soviet-dominated East and a US-aligned West.

blocks.text: From Stettin in the Baltic to Trieste in the Adriatic, an iron curtain has descended across the Continent.

Churchill's phrase captured a new reality: the USSR installed friendly **communist governments** across Eastern Europe - Poland, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, East Germany and others - creating a bloc of **satellite states**. Germany itself was divided into a democratic West and a communist East.

items: The 'curtain' was ideological, political and eventually physical.
Movement and information were tightly controlled between the two halves of Europe.
Berlin, deep inside East Germany, became the Cold War's most dangerous flashpoint.

slides.notes: Explain who Churchill was and that the speech is a famous primary source - useful for a source-analysis exercise on tone, purpose and audience. Set up the next slide by stressing Berlin's unusual position: a city split into Western and Soviet sectors, but located entirely within communist East Germany, making it a pressure point.

slides.heading: Berlin: blockade, airlift and wall
slides.lead: Divided Berlin became the front line where Cold War tensions repeatedly threatened to boil over.

blocks.text: In June 1948 the USSR **blockaded** West Berlin, cutting road and rail links to force the Western powers out. The West responded with the **Berlin Airlift**, flying in food and fuel for almost a year until the Soviets backed down in May 1949. In August 1961 East Germany built the **Berlin Wall** to stop its citizens fleeing to the West.

items: The airlift became a symbol of Western resolve; pilots from several countries, including South Africa, took part.
The Wall physically divided families and became the enduring image of a divided world.
Its fall in November 1989 later signalled the Cold War's end.

image.alt: A cargo aeroplane flying over a high wall that separates two halves of a city.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#cfe0ec'/><rect y='120' width='240' height='40' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='112' y='58' width='16' height='62' fill='#6b6b6b'/><rect x='108' y='52' width='24' height='8' fill='#4d4d4d'/><g fill='#2f4b7a'><path d='M40 44 L96 52 L96 62 L44 60 Z'/><path d='M56 40 L70 46 L62 52 Z'/><rect x='90' y='50' width='16' height='4'/></g><g fill='#7a5a3a'><rect x='30' y='96' width='26' height='24'/><rect x='170' y='96' width='26' height='24'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Use Berlin to make the abstract conflict concrete across three moments: blockade (1948-49), Wall (1961), fall (1989). Note SAHO's coverage of South African pilots in the Berlin Airlift as a local connection point (see sources). Emphasise the human cost of the Wall. This slide deliberately bookends the whole Cold War, which learners will study up to its end in Topic 6.

slides.heading: Key moments of the Cold War

blocks.alt: A timeline of Cold War events from 1945 to 1991.

events.label: Yalta and Potsdam conferences; wartime alliance breaks down
Korean War - the first major proxy war
Height of US involvement in Vietnam
Berlin Wall falls; USSR collapses; Cold War ends

slides.notes: Use this slide to consolidate chronology before moving into the nuclear arms race and the crises. Ask learners to identify the pattern of escalation and easing (tension, crisis, brief thaw, renewed tension). Point out the long span - over four decades - so they grasp that the Cold War shaped the entire lifetimes of their grandparents' generation.

slides.heading: Nuclear weapons and the balance of terror
slides.lead: Each superpower built enough weapons to destroy the other many times over - and that is what kept the peace.

blocks.text: The USA used atomic bombs in 1945; the USSR tested its own in 1949. Both then raced to build ever more powerful **nuclear arsenals** and the missiles to deliver them. This produced the grim logic of **Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD)**: because either side could annihilate the other in retaliation, neither dared strike first.

items: The competition spread to space - the USSR's **Sputnik** satellite (1957) shocked the USA.
**Deterrence** meant safety rested on the threat of total destruction.
The danger of accident or miscalculation made every crisis terrifying.

image.alt: A balance scale with a missile on each side, evenly weighted.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='116' y='40' width='8' height='90' fill='#3a3a3a'/><rect x='96' y='130' width='48' height='10' fill='#3a3a3a'/><rect x='50' y='48' width='140' height='6' fill='#3a3a3a'/><g fill='#2f4b7a'><path d='M56 96 l8 -34 l8 34 Z'/><rect x='60' y='96' width='8' height='10'/></g><g fill='#8a1f1f'><path d='M168 96 l8 -34 l8 34 Z'/><rect x='172' y='96' width='8' height='10'/></g><line x1='120' y1='54' x2='64' y2='62' stroke='#3a3a3a' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='120' y1='54' x2='176' y2='62' stroke='#3a3a3a' stroke-width='2'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the paradox at the centre of the Cold War: the weapons that made it most dangerous were also, through deterrence, what prevented a direct 'hot' war between the superpowers. Introduce MAD and deterrence carefully - these are examinable concepts. Note the wider costs: enormous military spending, public fear, and the growth of anti-nuclear movements.

slides.heading: The Cuban Missile Crisis
slides.lead: For thirteen days the world came closer to nuclear war than at any other time.

blocks.text: When the USSR secretly placed **nuclear missiles in Cuba**, just off the US coast, President **Kennedy** ordered a naval blockade and demanded their removal. After a tense standoff, Soviet leader **Khrushchev** agreed to withdraw the missiles; in return the USA secretly promised to remove its missiles from Turkey and not to invade Cuba.

items: It was the closest the Cold War came to direct nuclear conflict.
The crisis showed both the danger of brinkmanship and the value of back-channel diplomacy.
Afterwards a telephone 'hotline' linked Washington and Moscow to reduce the risk of accidental war.

image.alt: A clock face showing a few minutes to midnight, symbolising nearness to catastrophe.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='100' cy='80' r='56' fill='#fff' stroke='#3a3a3a' stroke-width='4'/><circle cx='100' cy='80' r='5' fill='#8a1f1f'/><line x1='100' y1='80' x2='100' y2='40' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4' stroke-linecap='round'/><line x1='100' y1='80' x2='120' y2='58' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4' stroke-linecap='round'/><g fill='#3a3a3a'><rect x='97' y='30' width='6' height='8'/><rect x='97' y='122' width='6' height='8'/><rect x='150' y='77' width='8' height='6'/><rect x='42' y='77' width='8' height='6'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This is the classic case study of Cold War brinkmanship. Walk learners through cause (Soviet missiles in Cuba, itself a response to US missiles in Turkey and the failed Bay of Pigs invasion), crisis (blockade and ultimatum), and resolution (secret deal). Stress that the public 'win' for Kennedy concealed mutual concessions - a good example of why we interrogate the official version of events.

slides.heading: Fighting through other countries
slides.lead: Unable to fight each other directly, the superpowers backed opposing sides in wars across the globe.

columns.text: Communist North Korea, backed by the USSR and China, invaded the US-backed South. A UN force intervened. The war ended in stalemate near the original border, leaving Korea divided to this day.

The USA fought a long, costly war to stop communist North Vietnam taking over the South. Despite huge force it withdrew in 1973, and Vietnam was reunified under communism in 1975 - a major setback for containment.

slides.notes: Define 'proxy war' clearly: the superpowers supplied weapons, money and advisers to local allies rather than fighting one another. Note that these wars were devastating for the countries concerned. Vietnam in particular damaged US confidence and prestige. Preview the next slide by noting many proxy conflicts unfolded in the newly independent 'Third World', including Southern Africa (Angola), which learners study in later topics.

slides.heading: Superpowers, blocs and the Non-Aligned
slides.lead: The whole world was pulled into a two-sided struggle - but not every nation wanted to choose a side.

blocks.text: The Cold War created a **bipolar order** in which most states aligned with either Washington or Moscow. As European empires collapsed after 1945, both superpowers competed for influence in newly independent nations across Africa and Asia, often through aid, trade and support for friendly governments.

items: The **Non-Aligned Movement** (formed 1961) let states such as India, Egypt and Yugoslavia refuse to join either bloc.
The 1955 **Bandung Conference** had already voiced this desire for independence from the superpowers.
Southern Africa, including the war in Angola, was drawn into this global contest - a link to later Grade 12 topics.

image.alt: Two large circles pulling smaller circles towards them, with some smaller circles staying in the middle.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 260 160' role='img'><rect width='260' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='46' cy='80' r='30' fill='#2f4b7a'/><circle cx='214' cy='80' r='30' fill='#8a1f1f'/><g fill='#7a8b5a'><circle cx='96' cy='54' r='9'/><circle cx='92' cy='104' r='9'/><circle cx='164' cy='50' r='9'/><circle cx='168' cy='108' r='9'/></g><g fill='#8a8f96'><circle cx='130' cy='72' r='10'/><circle cx='128' cy='104' r='8'/><circle cx='136' cy='40' r='7'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Explain that decolonisation and the Cold War overlapped: newly free nations became a battleground for superpower influence. The Non-Aligned Movement is important for South African learners because independent Africa's stance, and later the ANC's international alliances, connect to this bigger picture. Keep the framing responsible: many liberation movements had genuine local roots, not just Cold War sponsorship.

slides.heading: The Cold War: what we learned
slides.lead: A bipolar, ideological struggle - waged through crises, proxy wars and an arms race - that shaped the world without a direct superpower war.

blocks.text: From the breakdown of the wartime alliance in 1945 to the collapse of the USSR in 1991, the Cold War divided the globe between two superpowers and two ideologies. It was 'won' not on a battlefield but through economic strain, political change and the eventual fall of communism in Europe - remembered most vividly in the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989.

blocks.question: Explain why the wartime alliance between the USA and USSR broke down after 1945. Refer to both ideology and security concerns.
'The nuclear arms race made the Cold War both more dangerous and more stable.' Discuss this statement.
Using the Cuban Missile Crisis OR the Vietnam War as an example, explain what is meant by Cold War 'brinkmanship' or a 'proxy war'.

slides.notes: Close by tying the threads together: origins, ideology, crises, proxy wars and the bipolar order. Use the three questions as extended-writing practice; the mark allocations mirror CAPS essay and source-based styles. Signpost forward to Topic 6 (the end of the Cold War, 1989 onward) and to the Southern African dimension (Angola, the liberation struggle). Remind learners to evaluate the reliability and bias of Cold War sources, since both sides produced propaganda.
